guides.title: Dog Choking First Aid
guides.summary: Immediate steps to help a choking dog before reaching a vet.
guides.warning: Do NOT perform a blind finger sweep as this may push the object deeper. Stay calm to avoid panicking your dog.
steps: Restrain your dog gently but firmly to prevent biting.
Open the dog's mouth carefully and look inside for any visible object.
If you can see the object clearly, use your fingers or tweezers to carefully remove it.
If you cannot see or remove it, place both hands on the side of the rib cage and apply firm, quick pressure (Heimlich-style thrust).
Repeat thrusts up to 5 times and check the mouth after each attempt.
If the dog loses consciousness, begin rescue breathing: close the mouth and breathe into the nose until the chest rises.
Transport to a veterinarian immediately even if the object is removed.
next_steps: Contact your nearest veterinarian immediately.
Monitor your dog for breathing difficulties for at least 24 hours.

guides.title: Dog Poisoning First Aid
guides.summary: Steps to take if your dog has ingested a toxic substance.
guides.warning: Do NOT induce vomiting unless specifically instructed by a veterinarian. Some substances cause more damage coming back up.
steps: Stay calm and keep your dog as still and comfortable as possible.
Identify the suspected poison — collect the container, plant, or substance.
Do NOT give food, water, or try to induce vomiting without vet guidance.
Check your dog's breathing, pulse, and level of consciousness.
If the dog is unconscious or seizing, position it on its side to prevent choking.
Call your veterinarian or emergency animal poison control immediately.
Bring the suspected poison sample or packaging with you to the vet.
next_steps: Call Animal Poison Control at once.
Do not leave the dog unattended.

guides.title: Dog Heatstroke First Aid
guides.summary: Cool your dog down safely and prevent organ damage from overheating.
guides.warning: Do NOT use ice-cold water — this can cause shock. Do NOT cover the dog in wet towels for extended periods.
steps: Move the dog immediately to a cool, shaded, or air-conditioned area.
Pour cool (not ice cold) water over the dog, focusing on the neck, armpits, and groin.
Fan the dog while wetting to aid evaporative cooling.
Offer small amounts of cool water to drink if the dog is conscious and able to swallow.
Place the dog on a cool damp towel — do not wrap it.
Monitor rectal temperature if a thermometer is available; target below 39.5°C.
Transport to the vet as soon as possible even if the dog seems to recover.
next_steps: Seek immediate veterinary attention.
Heatstroke can cause organ failure even after apparent recovery.

guides.title: Cat Choking First Aid
guides.summary: Steps to help a choking cat safely.
guides.warning: Cats in distress may scratch or bite. Approach carefully. Do NOT perform a full Heimlich manoeuvre on a cat — it is too small and fragile.
steps: Keep calm — your panic will worsen your cat's distress.
Gently restrain the cat by wrapping it in a thick towel to prevent scratching.
Open the mouth carefully and look for any visible foreign object.
If you can see the object, carefully use tweezers to extract it. Never use your fingers blindly.
If you cannot remove it, hold the cat with its back against your chest and apply 5 gentle, firm thrusts to the abdomen with two fingers.
Check the mouth after each thrust.
If the cat becomes unconscious, begin rescue breaths into the nose.
Take to a veterinarian immediately.
next_steps: Seek urgent veterinary care even if the object is removed.
Monitor for laboured breathing or continued distress.

guides.title: Cat Poisoning First Aid
guides.summary: Cats are especially sensitive to many common household substances.
guides.warning: Do NOT give milk, salt water, or induce vomiting. Cats metabolise toxins differently and many common remedies are harmful.
steps: Remove the cat from the source of the poison immediately.
If poison is on the coat or skin, rinse the area thoroughly with warm water.
Do not allow the cat to groom itself — this reingests the toxin.
Collect and note the suspected substance, with the container if possible.
Keep the cat warm, calm and still.
Do NOT give food, water, or any home remedies.
Call your veterinarian or animal poison control immediately.
Transport to the vet with the suspected substance sample.
next_steps: Contact a vet or poison hotline immediately.
Keep the cat isolated and monitor for vomiting, tremors or collapse.

guides.title: Cat Bleeding First Aid
guides.summary: Manage external bleeding in cats while arranging urgent veterinary care.
guides.warning: Do NOT use a tourniquet unless it is a life-threatening limb bleed and no other option is available. Do NOT remove objects embedded in a wound.
steps: Wear gloves if available to protect yourself and reduce infection risk.
Apply gentle but firm pressure to the wound using a clean cloth or gauze.
Hold pressure continuously for at least 3–5 minutes without lifting the cloth.
If blood soaks through, add more cloth on top — do not remove the first layer.
Once bleeding slows, secure the dressing lightly with a bandage if possible.
Keep the cat as still and calm as possible.
Transport to a veterinarian immediately even if bleeding appears to have stopped.
next_steps: Veterinary attention is required for all significant wounds.
Watch for signs of shock: pale gums, rapid breathing, weakness.

guides.title: Rabbit Heatstroke First Aid
guides.summary: Rabbits are extremely vulnerable to heat — act fast.
guides.warning: Rabbits can die from heatstroke very rapidly. Do NOT submerge the rabbit in water. Do NOT use ice.
steps: Move the rabbit immediately to a cool, shaded indoor area.
Wet the ears gently with cool (not cold) water — this is the primary heat-exchange area in rabbits.
Place the rabbit on a damp cool towel.
Use a fan at a distance to promote gentle airflow.
Offer fresh cool water but do not force the rabbit to drink.
Mist the rabbit's body lightly with cool water but avoid wetting the face.
Transport to the vet immediately — rabbit heatstroke is life-threatening within minutes.
next_steps: Emergency vet visit is essential even if the rabbit seems better.
Rabbits hide illness — internal organ damage may not show immediately.

guides.title: Rabbit Fracture First Aid
guides.summary: Rabbit bones are fragile — handle with extreme care.
guides.warning: Do NOT attempt to splint or straighten the limb yourself. Improper handling can cause paralysis in rabbits with spinal injuries.
steps: Keep the rabbit as still and calm as possible — speak gently.
Do not allow the rabbit to jump or move freely.
Gently place the rabbit in a padded, enclosed box or carrier lined with a soft towel.
Minimise handling to reduce pain and risk of further injury.
Keep the rabbit warm with a light cover — not too hot.
Do not offer food or water if surgery may be required.
Transport to a veterinarian immediately and as smoothly as possible.
next_steps: Immediate veterinary care is essential.
Fractures in rabbits often require surgical intervention.

guides.title: Hamster Seizure First Aid
guides.summary: Steps to keep your hamster safe during and after a seizure.
guides.warning: Do NOT pick up the hamster during a seizure — it may bite reflexively and you may injure it. Do NOT put objects in its mouth.
steps: Stay calm and do not attempt to restrain the hamster.
Clear the area around the hamster to prevent it from injuring itself on cage objects.
Dim the lights and reduce noise in the room.
Time the duration of the seizure using a clock.
Once the seizure ends, the hamster may be confused or weak — this is normal.
Keep the hamster warm and quiet while it recovers.
If seizures last more than 2 minutes or recur, seek immediate veterinary care.
next_steps: Consult a vet even if the hamster appears recovered.
Record seizure duration and frequency to help the vet diagnose the cause.

guides.title: Hamster Bleeding First Aid
guides.summary: Managing bleeding in hamsters requires a very gentle approach.
guides.warning: Hamsters are tiny and fragile. Excessive pressure can cause further harm. Any significant bleeding in a hamster is a veterinary emergency.
steps: Remain calm and handle the hamster extremely gently.
Place a tiny piece of clean, sterile gauze or soft tissue over the wound.
Apply very light, gentle pressure — do not press hard.
Hold for 2–3 minutes without lifting the gauze.
If bleeding is from a limb, keep the limb elevated slightly.
Place the hamster in a clean, quiet, warm container.
Transport to a vet as quickly as possible.
next_steps: Urgent veterinary care is required for any bleeding hamster.
Do not attempt to clean or treat the wound beyond basic pressure.
